turns.speaker: D

extension to about a hundred and fifteen degrees as well as lacking about maybe twenty to thirty degrees of terminal flexion on the secondary secondary to the pain here and Scott do have a positive anterior cruciate ligament tear and Scott's heard of anterior cruciate ligament tear probably even heard anterior cruciate ligament tear as acl tear sometimes especially when a lot of people do sports and they injure their knee that that can happen so the doctor's plan for Scott though is the doctor wan na the doctor wan na go ahead and order an mri because the doctor wan na evaluate the integrity of that acl now Scott may need surgery but the doctor will the doctor will first take a look at the mri result and reevaluate and reevaluate the anterior cruciate ligament tear first but in the Scott to go ahead and ice the knee if Scott need to if Scott's still seeing some swelling and some pain Scott can go ahead and use ice as well but the doctor definitely would like for Scott to moderate Scott's activities so the doctor would like for Scott to wear a knee immobilizer and then also use crutches when Scott walk around just to help Scott a little bit so not to put too much stress and strain on that knee does that sound like a plan

The encounter is framed as a follow-up visit for Scott’s knee injury.
Scott reports he was playing pickup soccer games when the injury occurred.
Scott reports feeling a 'pop' in the right knee at the time of injury.
Scott reports taking ibuprofen on a regular basis and that it helped a little.
Scott reports that bending his leg hurts toward the end of the motion.
Scott reports he can straighten his leg but needs to help get it straightened, indicating difficulty with the straightening activity.
During the exam, ecchymosis of the right knee is noted as a finding associated with Scott.
Gait is assessed as part of the exam (noted as 'marked limp'), making this measurement/assessment associated with Scott.
The clinician explicitly states they will perform a physical exam of Scott’s knee and proceeds with it.
A marked limp of the right leg is observed during ambulation, indicating a right-leg-related problem affecting Scott.
Scott is observed ambulating (with a marked limp) during the gait assessment, linking him to the ambulation activity in this context.
Scott reports he can still bear weight on the right knee.
In response to the clinician’s question about walking, Scott indicates he can bear weight and is ambulating (though with limp), supporting that he is able to walk.
Patient reports pain localized to the inside of the right knee when examined/pressed.
